metadata.source: Species detail enrichment overlay for reserved plant batch c

records.culinary_uses: Nutmeats are shelled, ground or chopped, then leached to remove tannins before being used as flour, mush, pancakes, dumplings, roasted bits, or acorn meal for breads.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a calorie food rather than a medicine; high-tannin raw material also has traditional astringent use in some contexts.
records.notes: Only usable after thorough leaching. White oak acorns are usually less bitter than red oak acorns. Sound nuts need to be gathered quickly because insects, mold, and squirrels all move faster than your good intentions.

records.culinary_uses: Fragrant flowers and attached bracts are steeped for tea, infused in honey, used in syrups, or added lightly to desserts and cooling summer drinks.
records.medicinal_uses: Classic calming tea herb with long traditional use for colds, mild feverish states, and evening relaxation.
records.notes: Use blossoms while fresh and fragrant before they brown. Young basswood leaves are also edible, but this entry is focused on the flowers.

records.culinary_uses: Small sweet nutmeats can be eaten out of hand, roasted, added to baking, mixed into trail blends, or chopped into granola and stuffings when mast years are good.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a mast food rather than a medicine.
records.notes: Years vary wildly. Beechnut crops matter hugely to wildlife, so do not strip a patch clean. The husks are prickly and the nuts are small, but the flavor is excellent.

records.culinary_uses: Spring sap can be drunk fresh, reduced into syrup, fermented lightly, or used in tea and cooking. Birch syrup is excellent but takes a shocking amount of sap.
records.medicinal_uses: More of a fresh spring tonic drink than a strong medicinal plant.
records.notes: Tapped in late winter to early spring before leaf-out. Birch often gives good drinking sap even when making syrup is a patience test from hell.

records.culinary_uses: First-year roots are dug in fall or spring and cooked as a starchy, slightly sweet vegetable, often braised, stir-fried, or dried for broth and soup use.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional alterative and digestive root with long use in herbal practice.
records.notes: Best from first-year rosettes before the stalk shoots up. Mature second-year roots turn woody. Clean carefully because soil clings like it owes the root money.

records.culinary_uses: Bright yellow pollen is shaken from male flower spikes and used like a rich flour extender in pancakes, muffins, breads, noodles, and camp baking.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a food ingredient here; some folk use exists, but culinary value is the main point.
records.notes: Collect only from clean wetlands. Pollen harvest is brief and weather-sensitive. A tiny breeze can turn your harvest into airborne regret.

records.culinary_uses: Rhizomes can be roasted, boiled, pounded for starch, or scraped for flour-like material, though the processing is muddy and labor-heavy.
records.medicinal_uses: More survival-starch than medicine in this guide.
records.notes: Harvest only from clean water. Rhizomes contain a lot of fiber and mud, so they are more work than glamour food. Useful, yes. Romantic, not really.

records.culinary_uses: Tender inner shoots can be eaten raw or cooked and are often compared with cucumber or mild green corn. They work well sliced into salads or lightly sautéed.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a food plant; medicinal use is secondary here.
records.notes: Harvest the white inner core from clean marshes only. Do not confuse this with rhizome starch or pollen harvest; the plant does different jobs at different stages.

records.culinary_uses: Tender tops are eaten raw in salads, blended into pesto, or added late to soups and egg dishes for a cool moist green note.
records.medicinal_uses: Cooling folk herb often used for irritated skin and mildly inflamed conditions.
records.notes: Best while lush and young; older heat-stressed plants get stringy. Avoid polluted yards and dog-walk zones, because chickweed is blameless but its neighborhood may not be.

records.culinary_uses: Blue flowers are edible fresh in small amounts as garnish or infused color, but they are a minor edible compared with the root.
records.medicinal_uses: Flowers and aerial parts show up in mild bitter-tonic traditions.
records.notes: Pretty and edible, but not supper. Better treated as garnish, tea accent, or visual charm than as a major food.

records.culinary_uses: Roots are dug, cleaned, roasted, and ground for a coffee-like drink or blended with coffee. Young roots can also be cooked as a vegetable.
records.medicinal_uses: Classic bitter digestive root and gentle liver-support herb in Western herbalism.
records.notes: Best from first-year plants before the flower stalk develops. Roasting changes it from sharp bitter to pleasantly deep and toasty.

records.culinary_uses: Flowers are used for tea, dried flour extender, or small fresh additions to salads and baked goods. Best as an herbal ingredient rather than a bulk food.
records.medicinal_uses: Red clover especially has long traditional use as a tea herb for skin, lymphatic, and nutritive support.
records.notes: Use fresh sweet blossoms before they brown. The flowers are more useful dried than eaten by the handful.

records.culinary_uses: Flowers are used in fritters, wine, jelly, syrup, infused vinegar, and petal additions to batter or rice dishes.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a food flower, though the whole plant has much stronger medicinal use than the flower alone.
records.notes: Use fully open clean flowers. The green bases can add bitterness, so petal-only use often makes prettier, better-tasting recipes.

records.culinary_uses: Young spring leaves are eaten raw, wilted, sautéed, or added to soups; older leaves are better cooked to tame the bitterness.
records.medicinal_uses: Classic bitter digestive and diuretic herb, especially from leaf and root.
records.notes: Best before flowering or from shaded growth. Strongly bitter in heat and drought. It is one of the easiest real edible weeds to learn, which is probably why it keeps showing up in every foraging conversation ever.

records.culinary_uses: Buds and flowers can be stir-fried, stuffed, battered, dried for soups, or added to egg dishes. Buds are usually the best culinary stage.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly an edible ornamental escape rather than a medicinal plant in this guide.
records.notes: Use true daylily, not just any plant with 'lily' in the name. Some people get digestive upset, so small test portions are sensible instead of charging in like a Victorian goat.
